coredata.dc:title: Bridging environmental and biological monitoring: Constructing platform for hexavalent chromium detection and cancer-cells screening based on red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Hu, Jinshuang
Yao, Boxuan
Wei, Hua
Zhang, Caiyun
Zhou, Jiarui
Liu, Jian
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: Intensive research on biocompatible carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials provides a brand-new platform for environmental and biological monitoring, but its applicability is limited by the single signal response, unfriendly synthesis route, or rare red fluorescence performance. In this study, we designed and prepared a new type of high-efficiency red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (R-PDs) through rationally preferred raw materials. The proposed R-PDs with colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal response to hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) can be obtained easily by hydrothermal treatment of o-hydroxybenzoic acid and p-phenylenediamine. A high-throughput smartphone-mediated scanometric analysis platform was constructed based on sensitive gray change, which was further applied for the rapid, simple, and low-cost detection of Cr(VI) in real environmental water. The fluorescent analysis mode exhibits high sensitivity with the limit of detection 20 nM, thereby realizing the Cr(VI) imaging in living cells and aquatic species. More importantly, R-PDs exhibited excellent nucleolus targeting capacity by virtue of their inherent high nitrogen content, thus successfully screening normal and cancerous cells. Encouraged by the simplicity of synthesis and availability of constructed analysis platform, the proposed R-PDs has great potential applications in environmental monitoring, biosensing and biomedical.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Dual-signal response
Cr(VI) detection
Nucleolus targeting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of Dual-mode Cr(Ⅵ) detection system
Imaging Cr(Ⅵ) in living cells, Daphnia Magna and zebrafish

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–visible absorption, excitation and emission spectrum of R-PDs (a), the TEM image (b), size distribution (c), XRD pattern (d), FTIR spectrum of R-PDs (e), high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (f), N1s (g) and O1s (h) of R-PDs.

The UV–visible absorption (a) and PL spectra (Excitation wavelength: 550 nm) (b) of R-PDs before and after Cr(Ⅵ) added, inset: photographs of corresponding optical and fluorescent (under 365 nm UV light) changes. The UV–visible absorption (c) and PL spectra (f) of R-PDs with different concentrations Cr(Ⅵ). Plots of A-A0 (d) and (I0-I)/I0 (g) vs the concentration of Cr(Ⅵ) based on absorption and fluorescent mode respectively. Absorbance (e) and PL intensity (h) response of R-PDs toward various metal ions (100 μM).

The home-made test strip for Cr(VI) rapid detection (a), scanometric analysis platform of Cr(Ⅵ) based on a 96-well plate (b), the linear curve for Cr(Ⅵ) detection with dependence the gray value change (c), selectivity towards common cations interference (d).

Fluorescence microscopy images (a1-e1) and their corresponding bright-field transmission images (a2-e2) and merged images (a3-e3) of Hela cells. Hela cells treated just with R-PDs (a1-a3), and the Hela cell were preincubated with Cr(Ⅵ) and then incubated with R-PDs (b-e). Cr(Ⅵ) concentration: 5 μM (b1-b3), 15 μM (c1-c3), 25 μM (d1-d3), 35 μM (e1-e3). Effect of Cr(Ⅵ) concentration on the PL intensity in Hela cells (f), and the linear curve between PL intensities and Cr(Ⅵ) concentrations (g). All images were taken at green light excitation.

Imaging of Cr(Ⅵ) in zebrafish at different development phases, day 0 (a), day 1 (b), day 2 (c), and day 3 (d). Blank refers to the zebrafish treated just using R-PDs. For Cr(Ⅵ) detection, the zebrafish embryos and larvae were preincubated with different concentrations Cr(Ⅵ) (10 or 30 μM), and then treated with R-PDs. All images were taken at green light excitation.
Fluorescence images of Hela cells treated with R-PDs (a) and SYTO RNA select (b). Merged image of (a) and (b) (c). Intensities of the selected region in the green channel and red channel (d).
Fluorescence images of Hela and MCF-10A cells treated by R-PDs (a,b), frequency distributions of the number of nucleolus per cell for MCF-10A and Hela cell lines (c).
Illustration of PDs synthesis and application of Cr(VI) detection and nucleolus targeting imaging.
Determination of Cr(Ⅵ) in real water samples by Smartphone mediated scanometric analysis method.

head.ce:title: Bridging environmental and biological monitoring: Constructing platform for hexavalent chromium detection and cancer-cells screening based on red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330022, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors, Xin Wang and Jinshuang Hu contributed equally to this manuscript and should be considered as co-first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intensive research on biocompatible carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials provides a brand-new platform for environmental and biological monitoring, but its applicability is limited by the single signal response, unfriendly synthesis route, or rare red fluorescence performance. In this study, we designed and prepared a new type of high-efficiency red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (R-PDs) through rationally preferred raw materials. The proposed R-PDs with colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal response to hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) can be obtained easily by hydrothermal treatment of o-hydroxybenzoic acid and p-phenylenediamine. A high-throughput smartphone-mediated scanometric analysis platform was constructed based on sensitive gray change, which was further applied for the rapid, simple, and low-cost detection of Cr(VI) in real environmental water. The fluorescent analysis mode exhibits high sensitivity with the limit of detection 20 nM, thereby realizing the Cr(VI) imaging in living cells and aquatic species. More importantly, R-PDs exhibited excellent nucleolus targeting capacity by virtue of their inherent high nitrogen content, thus successfully screening normal and cancerous cells. Encouraged by the simplicity of synthesis and availability of constructed analysis platform, the proposed R-PDs has great potential applications in environmental monitoring, biosensing and biomedical.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are a type of non-degradable persistent pollutants with high environmental and biological risks, and have always been the focus of research in the fields of environmental science and bioscience [1–3]. Among them, carcinogenic hexavalent chromium (Cr(Ⅵ)) has been one of the most concerned toxic heavy metals contaminants due to its extensive use in industrial fields, such as leather tanning, chromium plating, electroplating, etc [4,5]. The hazards of Cr(VI) are not only reflected in cell damage, but also in bioaccumulation due to the amplification of the food chain [6]. According to the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), the maximum allowable limit of Cr(VI) in drinking water is 100 μg·L-1 [7]. Therefore, the effective detection of Cr(VI) in environmental and biological systems is of great significance to ensure the safety of drinking water sources and biological species.

Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal response method was highly advocated recently that can simultaneously satisfy the requirements of simple quantitative and in vivo imaging analysis of target analyte in the environment and biosensing field [8,9]. Compared to conventional Cr(VI) analysis methods [10–13], the colorimetric method is extremely suitable for long-term tracking of the level changes of Cr(VI) in the aquatic environment and for responding to emergencies of Cr(VI) pollution due to the advantages of cost-effective and simple operation, and in-situ availability [14–16]. And fluorescent method with high sensitivity, fast response, in-situ imaging and non-invasive, has been considered as an efficient approach for imaging Cr(VI) in vivo [17–19]. As the core of the analysis method, various materials have been applied for the colorimetric or fluorescent detection of Cr(VI), such as organic molecules [20], metal nanoparticles [21], semiconductor quantum dots [22], and metal–organic frameworks [23], etc. These materials are all effective but still suffer from complex synthetic routes, high cost, low biocompatibility, and high secondary pollution risk [24]. Besides, simultaneous colorimetric and fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) based on a single sensing substrate is still difficult to achieve. Hence, the development of new functional optical materials with low cost, biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness for simultaneously realizing colorimetric and fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) is of great significance.

Cancer prediction and treatment has also been a hotspot issue in the biomedical field with the increasing abuse and discharge of Cr(VI) and other carcinogens [25–27]. Nucleolus targeted tracking was considered to be vital means for tumor prediction [28], and nucleolus is also deemed to be an effective target for cancer therapy [29]. In this sense, the development of novel nucleolus dyes with low-cost and better biocompatibility is urgent to overcome the shortage of the only commercial probe SYTO RNA Select, such as expensive, harsh storage conditions, and complex synthesis and staining procedures [30], which is contributing to the reasonable early prediction of the malignant tumor.

Carbon dots (CDs) have aroused great concern and been widely applied for environmental and biological analysis fields recent years due to their fascinating attributes like good biocompatibility, eco-friendliness, unique optical properties, abundant functional groups, cost-effectiveness, and so far [31–34]. As a new brand of CDs, carbonized polymer dots (PDs) with low carbonization degree are more suitable for sensing applications because of their better compatibility and easier modification in virtue of the rich functional groups on the surface [35–36]. Even though excellent fluorescent quench or enhancement of PDs can be induced by various target analytes [37], meaningful colorimetric signal response to analytes based on PDs is still difficult to achieve, for which integrating extra sensing systems are needed [38–40]. More importantly, the synthesis of long fluorescent emission PDs, which is more suitable for biological imaging analysis, is mostly restricted by the participation of organic solvents or a mass of inorganic acids [41–43]. Hence, it is still a challenge to develop facile preparation route for synthesizing long-wavelength PDs with sensitive response to specific analyte.

Inspired by the outstanding attributes of PDs and aim to improve the deficiencies of PDs in synthesis and properties to satisfy the urgent demand of environmental and biological analysis, herein, we designed and synthesized a new type of red fluorescence PDs (R-PDs) by rationally optimizing raw materials. The R-PDs with perfect optical properties were obtained easily just through hydrothermal treatment of o-hydroxybenzoic acid and p-phenylenediamine without organic solvent and inorganic acid. The prepared R-PDs exhibits unique dual-signal response to Cr(VI) both in colorimetric and fluorescent mode, which can be acted as an effective sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) in drinking water sources and biological species (Scheme 1 ). Based on that, a scanometric analysis platform mediated by smart-phone was constructed to realize convenient field testing by using the 96-well plate as high-throughput color array. Encouraged by the well fluorescence performance and sensitivity of that to Cr(VI), the R-PDs was successfully applied for imaging Cr(VI) in cells and aquatic species. Moreover, the developed R-PDs also possess superior nucleolus targeting character and was further used for the screening of normal and cancerous cells. We expect that this study could facilitate the development of high-efficiency PDs to realize reasonable environmental monitoring and biosensing applications.

O-hydroxybenzoic acid (salicylic acid), m-hydroxybenzoic acid, p-hydroxybenzoic acid, o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, catechol, phthalic acid, 8-hydroxyquinoline, citric acid, sodium chromate (Na2CrO4) and Hoechst 33342 were purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). P-phenylenediamine was purchased from Saan Chemical Technology Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium citrate was purchased from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Plant (Tianjin, China). SYTO RNA Select was purchased from Life Technologies (USA). All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without any further purification. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ·cm (UPR-II-40L, Sichuan, China) was used in the whole experiment.

The synthesis of R-PDs was optimized systemically aim to high-efficiency analysis applications and carried out using the hydrothermal method. In a typical process, 0.1 g p-phenylenediamine and 0.51 g o-hydroxybenzoic acid were dissolved in 30 mL water. Then, the resulted mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 3 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the products were centrifuged (8000 r/min, 10 min) and the liquid supernatant was collected and dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off 500). Then the products were freeze-dried for further application, which was named R-PDs. Control experiments also were performed through a similar process, the products obtained by the reaction of single p-phenylenediamine or o-hydroxybenzoic acid reaction were named PDs-1 and PDs-2 respectively. To investigate the effect of hydroxybenzoic acid spatial structure on the products, m-hydroxybenzoic acid and p-hydroxybenzoic acid also were used to react with p-phenylenediamine for preparing PDs, which are named m-PDs and p-PDs.

As a typical procedure, different concentrations Cr(VI) (10 μL each) solutions were added to a phosphate buffer solution (0.01 M, pH = 4) contained R-PDs with a concentration of 40 μg·mL−1 and 400 μg·mL−1 for colorimetric and fluorescent method respectively. The absorbances of 550 nm and PL intensities of 595 nm (Excitation wavelength: 550 nm) were measured after incubation for 8 min. The selectivity of the sensor with other metal ions and anions including Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, HCO3 –/CO3 2–, ClO-, Cl-, SO3 2-, NO2 –, C2O4 2-, F-, CH3COO–, SO4 2-, HS-/S2-, NO3 –, H2PO4 -/HPO4 2- were determined as similar procedure to Cr(VI). The practicability of designed detection platform for the analysis of Cr(VI) was evaluated through real water sample analysis and cell, Daphnia Magna and zebrafish imaging.

Novel R-PDs with dual-response to Cr(VI) was developed through hydrothermal treatment of o-hydroxybenzoic acid and p-phenylenediamine. The synthesis of R-PDs was systematically explored based on fluorescence efficiency and Cr(VI) response intensity. Firstly, control experiments were carried out to illustrate the benefic effect of simultaneous reaction. As shown in Figure S1a,b, the PDs-1 and PDs-2, which was obtained by just using p-phenylenediamine and o-hydroxybenzoic acid as reactant respectively, cannot show obvious signal response to Cr(VI) in UV–visible absorption spectra. And the quantum yield (QY) and fluorescent signal response of R-PDs (15.4 %) is superiorly to that of PDs-1 (1.91 %) and PDs-2 (0.30 %) (Figure S1c,d). Furthermore, as shown in Figure S2, the o-hydroxybenzoic acid can react with o-, m-, and p-phenylenediamine to obtain yellow (570 nm), green (520 nm), and red fluorescence (595 nm) products, respectively, and PDs with a long emission wavelength are preferred considering the requirement of biosensing [44]. Besides, the influence of the space structure of hydroxybenzoic acid on the product performance also was surveyed. From Figure S3a we can see the QYs of m-PDs (1.05 %) and p-PDs (0.89 %) obtained by m- and p-hydroxybenzoic acid reacting with p-phenylenediamine are much lower than R-PDs, and the absorbance signal response to Cr(VI) of R-PDs is much more sensitive than that of m-PDs and p-PDs (Figure S3b,c). All the above results indicate that the reaction of o-hydroxybenzoic acid and p-phenylenediamine is unique and synthesized R-PDs is most effective for Cr(VI) detection.

Furthermore, the role of functional groups in the synthesis and response to Cr(VI) of R-PDs was evaluated in detail. As shown in Figure S4, the o-hydroxybenzoic acid exhibits superior performance to phthalic acid and catechol, proving the joint effect of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. From Figure S4b and f we can see that the carboxyl groups involved can improve the PL efficiency of R-PDs but subject to lower signal response to Cr(VI). The QYs of products obtained by single p-phenylenediamine or reacting with phthalic acid were 1.91 % and 22.5 %, respectively. Conversely, hydroxyl group mainly contributes to improving the response sensitivity and is impotent for red fluorescence emerging of R-PDs (Figure S4c,g). The red fluorescence QYs of obtained products from different reaction systems related to this article were summarized in Table S1, the R-PDs possesses obvious advantage in photoluminescence (PL) efficiency over others. Therefore, we suspect that the red fluorescent of R-PDs should be attributed to the effective polymerization of amino group in p-phenylenediamine and carboxyl group in o-hydroxybenzoic acid, and the signal response to Cr(VI) might be correlated with the structure and surface active amino and hydroxyl functional groups of R-PDs [41].

The reaction conditions of R-PDs also were optimized. The R-PDs synthesized by the mass ratio of p-phenylenediamine and o-hydroxybenzoic acid at 1:4 exhibits optimal signal response and relatively high PL performance (Figure S5a1-a3). Furthermore, the optimal reaction time and temperature is 3 h and 180–200 ℃, respectively, which can be concluded easily from Figure S5b1-b3 and c1-c3. Besides, the synthetic route of R-PDs also has better efficiency and eco-friendliness, and lower cost compared to previous reports, as shown in Table S2.

The synthesized R-PDs was characterized in detail. Two peaks at 270 and 295 nm (Fig. 1 a) in the UV–visible absorption spectrum correspond to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains of R-PDs [45–46]. Besides, the broad absorption band around 520 nm in the lower-energy region can excite R-PDs, which could be attributed to the specific surface states of R-PDs [47]. The R-PDs aqueous solution can emit bright red fluorescent with optimal excitation and emission wavelength at 570 nm and 595 nm (Fig. 1a), respectively; and the emission peak is independent of excitation wavelength change due to the uniform surface states (Figure S6) [48]. Furthermore, the fluorescence of R-PDs not only can be maintained in most solvents (Figure S7), but also show good salt tolerance and photobleaching resistance (Figure S8).

The R-PDs is mono-dispersed with size distribution of 2–4.5 nm (Fig. 1b,c). In the XRD pattern (Fig. 1d), the R-PDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak centered at 20° and indicated an amorphous structure [49]. The characteristic absorption of functional groups in FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1e) including 3420 cm−1, 3325 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, 1509 cm−1, and 1292 cm−1 for ν (NH), ν (OH), ν (CO), ν (aromatic CC), and ν (CN), respectively, confirmed rich oxygenous and nitrogenous functional groups on R-PDs [50]. XPS was performed to further elucidate the chemical composition and surface functional groups of R-PDs. From Figure S9 we can observe the typical peaks of C (284.7 eV), N (399.6 eV), and O (531.5 eV) elements with the contents of 74.41 %, 14.01 %, and 11.21 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum indicates the presence of CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN/CO (285.9 eV), and CO (288.7 eV) (Fig. 1f). As shown in Fig. 1g, the N1s band can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.3 eV, 399.9 eV, and 401.1 eV, representing pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively [51]. In the O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 1h), two typical peaks can be observed at 531.3 eV (CO) and 532.5 eV (CO). The detailed values (%) of peak fitted were shown in Table S3. The XPS results correspond well with the FITR data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of Dual-mode Cr(Ⅵ) detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The designed R-PDs can be as effective sensor for Cr(Ⅵ) detection both in colorimetric and fluorescent mode. As shown in Fig. 2 a, almost colorless R-PDs aqueous solution exhibits a weak absorption at 550 nm, which increased with the solution color change to obvious violet after adding Cr(Ⅵ) (Fig. 2a inset). Encouragingly, in Fig. 2b, the red fluorescence of R-PDs shows more sensitive response to Cr(VI), and the PL intensity at 595 nm decreased sharply after Cr(VI) added. Therefore, the visible light colorimetry based on absorption change can be readily applied for field detection without the aid of extra exciting light, and the fluorescent method is more suitable for trace detection. The sensing conditions were optimized systemically, including pH, temperature and response time. From Figure S10a and d, we can conclude that the optimal sensing pH of dual-mode detection is about 4–5. The proposed analysis methods are robust to temperature change in a wide range of 0–60 ℃, which has a potential for Cr(VI) detection in extreme environmental conditions (Figure S10b,e). In addition, in Figure S10c and f, the signals of R-PDs change rapidly at first and plateaued after 8 min.

The analytical performance of constructed dual-mode detection system based on R-PDs was evaluated in detail. First, as shown in Fig. 2c, the absorption intensity of R-PDs at 550 nm increased gradually with adding different concentrations Cr(Ⅵ). The limit of detection (LOD) can reach 0.12 μM with a linear range of 0.5–100 μM (r = 0.9957) (Fig. 2d). For the fluorescent method, the PL intensity of R-PDs decreased gradually with increasing Cr(Ⅵ) concentrations (Fig. 2f), and the fluorescent mode possesses higher sensitivity with LOD at 20 nM, and the linear range is 0.1–30 μM (r = 0.9926) (Fig. 2g). The analytical performance of proposed methods was comparable or even superior to previously reported methods of Cr(Ⅵ) detection as shown in Table S4. Furthermore, the proposed methods also show excellent specificity for Cr(Ⅵ) detection. As shown in Fig. 2e,h and Figure S11, only Cr(Ⅵ) can induce distinct color change and fluorescence quenching, and other common cations and anions cannot lead to obvious signal response. Even if the concentration of common cations is 10-fold higher than Cr(VI), the constructed dual-mode detection system also shows high selectivity (Figure S12).

The possible mechanism of R-PDs to recognize Cr(Ⅵ) also was discussed through spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime experiments. In a previous report, the amino groups of CDs obtained from solvothermal treatment 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid can be oxidized to nitro groups by ClO- to cause spectral signal change, and ascorbic acid can reverse the process [52]. However, in our system, as shown in Figure S13, the ClO- cannot induce the R-PDs to generate purple signal, which indicates that the absorption signal change of R-PDs solution after Cr(Ⅵ) involved is not mainly derived from the oxidation of Cr(Ⅵ) in acid condition. Furthermore, in Figure S13, as a chelating agent, cysteine (Cys) can detach the Cr(Ⅵ) from the surface of R-PDs based on the stronger binding ability of Cr(Ⅵ) to Cys [41]. Therefore, we suspect the absorption change is mainly due to the generation of R-PDs-Cr(VI) complex based on coordination interaction. The original FTIR spectrum (Figure S14) of R-PDs shows the characteristic absorption band of NH, OH and CO stretching vibrations at 3420, 3325 and 1650 cm−1. However, after Cr(VI) added, these abovementioned bands decreased obviously, which confirmed that the surface active groups can effectively combine with Cr(VI) to form a complex [53,54]. From this point of view, the fluorescence quenching of R-PDs by Cr(VI) should be owned to charge transfer from the excited state of the PDs to Cr(VI). In addition, as shown in Figure S15 and Table S5, the average fluorescence lifetime of R-PDs before and after adding Cr(VI) is 2.11 and 2.17 ns, respectively, which suggests that the fluorescence quenching type of the R-PDs is a static quenching process in the presence of Cr(VI) because of the formation of the R-PDs-Cr(VI) complex [55]. Besides, the internal filtration effect (IFE) also occurred in the process due to the effective overlap of absorption and emission spectra of R-PDs after Cr(VI) involved, as shown in Figure S16. As displayed in Figure S17, the percentage of IFE mechanism for Cr(VI) to R-PDs is about 47.5 %, which was calculated based on the principles and equations [56].

For convenient field testing, we developed colorimetric test strip by loading R-PDs onto filter paper for the rapid judgment of Cr(Ⅵ) concentration. From Fig. 3 a we can see that the color of colorimetric test paper gradually changes from colorless to dark purple with increasing the concentrations of Cr(Ⅵ), which has a potential for rough in-situ sample detection. Although the test paper colorimetry possesses the advantages of simplicity and low cost, it’s still suffering from sensitivity and accurate quantification. And the absorption spectrometry cannot get rid of the dependence of the instrument. Therefore, simple, fast, accurate, and low-cost analysis methods have more development value and application prospects. Based on color changes visible to the naked eye, a smartphone mediated scanometric analysis platform of Cr(Ⅵ) was constructed by using a 96-well plate as the color array. As shown in Figure S18, the standard curve drawing and real sample detection were integrated into the same plate, which not only can effectively eliminate the image difference induced by phone model and camera parameters, but also can realize high-throughput analysis. Furthermore, a portable and low-cost device for capturing high-quality images was self-built, which contains an astral lamp, paper shade box, and photo capture channel. The rated power of astral lamp is 16 W and the correlated color temperature (CCT) is 6500 K. Then the photograph was taken by a Smartphone and the gray value was readout by Image J. A good linear curve between the gray value and Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 0.5–100 μM was obtained (r = 0.9970), and the color gradient also can be visually observed from Fig. 3c inset (Left to right, top to bottom). The detection limit can reach 0.12 μM and much lower than the limit value of Cr(VI) (∼2 μM) in the ground and drinking water [7]. Besides, the selectivity of scanometric analysis method also was researched through reading the gray values of obtained images after adding different metal ions, and only Cr(VI) can cause obvious gray value change, which indicated a satisfactory specific test (Fig. 3d).

The constructed platform was further applied for the fast, low-cost, and accurate detection of Cr(VI) in real water samples. The original concentrations of Cr(VI) in these samples are all lower than the LOD, and spiked experiments with different levels were performed to verify the practicality of proposed method. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries ranged from 95.2 % to 106.2 %, and the RSDs are less than 6 %. These results prove that the analysis platform is robust and reliable to detect Cr(VI) in real environmental water samples. Besides, the feasibility of the fluorescent method for more sensitive detection of Cr(VI) also was verified through spiked experiments as shown in Table S6. Real water samples with spiked Cr(VI) concentrations of 0.5, 5, and 15 μM were all successfully determined with recoveries in the range of 92.0–106.0 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging Cr(Ⅵ) in living cells, Daphnia Magna and zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent spectroscopic properties and fluorescent response to Cr(Ⅵ) of R-PDs, the developed R-PDs were further applied for the intracellular and in vivo imaging of Cr(Ⅵ). Firstly, in Figure S19, more than 93 % of the cells can remain perfect viability even with the concentration of R-PDs at 800 μg·mL−1 from MTT assay. The designed R-PDs exhibits better biocompatibility than some reported CDs as shown in Table S7. From Fig. 4 a we can see that the R-PDs can diffuse into cells easily and exhibit bright red fluorescence under the excitation of green light. Through different concentrations Cr(Ⅵ) treated, the red fluorescence in the intracellular region decreased gradually (Fig. 4b-e). Furthermore, the PL intensity of each image was read by Image J, and a linear curve between PL intensity and Cr(Ⅵ) concentration was obtained in the range of 5–35 μM (Fig. 4f), with a determination coefficient of 0.9988 (Fig. 4g). The above results suggest that the R-PDs can be an effective sensor for the intracellular imaging of Cr(Ⅵ).

Daphnia Magna and zebrafish with different development states were selected to verify the ability of R-PDs for Cr(Ⅵ) detection in vivo. As shown in Figure S20, the R-PDs can easily enter Daphnia Magna and the intestinal region emits obvious red fluorescence signal, while only weak fluorescence signals were recorded when the Daphnia Magna were pretreated with different concentrations Cr(Ⅵ). As shown in Fig. 5 , the R-PDs can infiltrate into zebrafish embryos and larvae readily, and an intensively red fluorescence can be observed, which proves that R-PDs can be as an efficient bioimaging material. R-PDs have different levels of affinity for different tissues. At 0 dpf, R-PDs enter yolk sac via the chorion (Fig. 5a), while it accumulated in abdomen of zebrafish embryo at 1–2 dpf (Fig. 5b,c). Accumulation of R-PDs within the zebrafish larval was evident in kidney at 3 dpf (Fig. 5d). These results confirm that R-PDs has high permeability and can pass through physical barriers. Through pretreated with Cr(Ⅵ), the red fluorescence in developing zebrafish decreased in a gradient with increasing the Cr(Ⅵ) concentration from 10 μM to 30 μM, which concludes that Cr(Ⅵ) can affect the zebrafish from the embryonic stage. These results indicate that the designed R-PDs-based probe is promising for the determination of exogenous Cr(Ⅵ) in vivo.

A large number of reported literature suggest that higher N-element content is conducive to achieving effective combination between optical materials and nucleolus in the cells [57,58]. Cell stain experiments primarily indicate that the synthesized R-PDs possesses the ability of targeting nucleoli due to inherent high-nitrogen content (14.01 %) of R-PDs. To further prove the nucleolus targeting capacity of R-PDs, the co-positioning experiments were performed using the nucleolar localization commercial dye SYTO RNA Select and R-PDs. As shown in Fig. 6 a-d, the fluorescence emission bands of R-PDs and SYTO RNA Select are partially overlapping with a high Pearson’s coefficient of 0.89, which confirmed the high nucleolus-targeting accuracy of R-PDs.

It is well known that most ribonucleic acids (especially rRNA) are gathered in nucleolus [59]. In order to study the R-PDs nucleolus targeting mechanism, DNase and RNase digestion experiments were carried out to study the effect of DNase and RNase on the fluorescence behavior of R-PDs in Hela cells. As shown in Figure S21a, bright nucleolus is observed in Hela cells after staining with R-PDs in the control group. After treatment with DNase, the fluorescence signal and distribution in the nucleolus is not changed significantly (Figure S21b). However, after RNase treatment, the characteristics of the nucleolus disappeared with the significant fluorescence intensity reduced and uniform fluorescence distribution, which indicates that the nucleolus targeting property results from the specific binding of R-PDs to RNA (Figure S21c). The specific targeting ability of R-PDs to RNA should be attributed to the difference of electrostatic interaction intensity. Due to abundant nitrogenous functional groups, the R-PDs is positively charged with the zeta potential of + 16.8 mV. Under the acid environment, the surface negative charges of RNA are much more than DNA because the isoelectric point of DNA (4–4.5) is higher than RNA (2–2.5) [60]. Therefore, based on electrostatic interaction [61], the R-PDs exhibit higher combining capacity to RNA over DNA.

Inspired by the excellent nucleolus-targeting capacity of R-PDs, the developed R-PDs was further applied for screening cancerous and normal cells based on the difference of nucleolus features [62]. The MCF-10A and Hela cells were treated with R-PDs, as shown in Fig. 7 a,b, the average number of nucleoli per cell was 1.51 and 4.02 corresponding to MCF-10A and Hela cells, respectively (Fig. 7c). The statistical analysis results suggest a significant difference in normal cells and cancerous cells. These results strongly demonstrate that R-PDs possesses high potential for early cancer diagnosis in the cellular level based on high-efficiency nucleolus targeting performance.

In this study, we rationally designed and prepared unique R-PDs with dual signal response to Cr(VI) and perfect nucleolus-targeting capacity. The established high-throughput scanometric analysis platform mediated by Smartphone and 96-well plate was successfully applied for the simple, low-cost and rapid analysis of Cr(Ⅵ) in the water environment. Furthermore, the fluorescent sensing system was used for the Cr(Ⅵ) imaging in living cells and aquatic species reasonably and exhibited excellent application prospects for biological analysis. More importantly, the R-PDs possesses the characteristics of stably and specifically highlighting nucleoli, further realizing the effective screening of cancerous and normal cells. The main advantages of presented research are the facile and eco-friendly synthesis of red fluorescence PDs and the convenience and high-efficacy applications of real-time environmental monitoring and clinical diagnosis analysis, which may provide a valuable reference for the application of CDs-based fluorescent nanomaterials in the environmental and biomedical field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the ﬁnancial support from the projects of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21806189, 22106054). Basic Research Project of Science, Education and Industry Integration Engineering Grant number: 2022PY073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.138524.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biocompatible carbon dots with low-saturation-intensity and high-photobleaching-resistance for STED nanoscopy imaging of the nucleolus and tunneling nanotubes in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, S. Ye, J. Q. Guo, H. B. Wang, W. Yan, J. Song, J. L. Qu, Biocompatible carbon dots with low-saturation-intensity and high-photobleaching-resistance for STED nanoscopy imaging of the nucleolus and tunneling nanotubes in living cells, Nano Res. 12 (2019) 3075-3084.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red emissive two-photon carbon dots: Photodynamic therapy in combination with real-time dynamic monitoring for the nucleolus

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Z. Yi, S. M. Deng, X. L. Guo, C. C. Pang, J. Y. Zeng, S. Ji, C. H. Liang, X. C. Shen, Red emissive two-photon carbon dots: Photodynamic therapy in combination with real-time dynamic monitoring for the nucleolus, Carbon 182 (2021) 155-166.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of carbon dots in nucleolus imaging to distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Y. Zhang, Q. Q. Duan, H. C. Zhao, Y. X. Zhang, X. M. Li, Y. F. Xi, Z. F. Wu, Z. F. Wu, L. Guo, P. C. Li, S. B. Sang, Application of carbon dots in nucleolus imaging to distinguish cancerous cells from normal cells, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 329 (2021) 129156.
